title: Raising Exceptions
subtitle: Signal errors from your own functions intentionally
learning_objectives: Use raise to trigger an exception intentionally
Validate function inputs and raise errors for invalid data
Choose the right built-in exception type to raise
Understand when to raise vs when to return a special value

sections.heading: The raise Statement
sections.content: You can raise an exception yourself using the raise keyword. This is useful when your function receives invalid input and you want to signal that clearly. Choose the exception type that best describes the problem.
sections.code_output:
Age set to 25
ValueError: Age cannot be negative

sections.heading: Raising Inside Functions for Validation
sections.content: A common pattern is to validate inputs at the top of a function and raise early if something is wrong. This is called a guard clause. It keeps the rest of the function clean and simple.
sections.code_output:
4.0
ValueError: Cannot take square root of negative number
sections.note: Common choices: raise ValueError for bad values, raise TypeError for wrong types, raise KeyError for missing keys.

exercises.title: Validate a Username
exercises.prompt: Write a function validate_username(name) that raises ValueError if the name is shorter than 3 characters or longer than 20 characters. If valid, print 'Username accepted: ' followed by the name. Test with validate_username("Al"), validate_username("Alice").
hints: If no exception, print 'Username accepted: ' + name

exercises.title: Guard Clause in a Function
exercises.prompt: Write a function divide(a, b). If b is 0, raise ZeroDivisionError with message 'Divider cannot be zero'. Otherwise return a / b. Print the result of divide(20, 4) and catch the error for divide(10, 0).
hints: Check if b == 0 and raise ZeroDivisionError
The except block will catch the error you raised

quiz.question: What is the main reason to raise exceptions in your own functions?
options: To make the program crash on purpose
To signal invalid input or impossible operations to the caller
quiz.explanation: Raising exceptions is how functions communicate to their callers that something is wrong. The caller can then catch and handle the error. This is much cleaner than returning special values like -1 or None and hoping the caller checks them.

quiz.question: Which exception should you raise for an invalid value (like a negative age)?
quiz.explanation: ValueError is the right choice when the type is correct (it is a number) but the value is invalid (negative). TypeError is for when the type itself is wrong (e.g. received a string instead of a number).

completion_message: Well done! You now know how to raise exceptions to signal problems clearly from your own functions.
